## Data Quality Classification

### Category A — Verified Historical Records:
- Painting dimensions and dates: museum websites (louvre.fr, moma.org, mauritshuis.nl, uffizi.it)
- Auction prices (specific sales): Christie's (christies.com), Sotheby's (sothebys.com) official press releases
- Artist biographical dates: Oxford Art Online / Grove Art, Encyclopaedia Britannica
- Nobel Prize winners: Nobel Prize Foundation (nobelprize.org) — authoritative, updated annually
- Box office records: Box Office Mojo (boxofficemojo.com), The Numbers (the-numbers.com)
- Classical music premieres: Grove Music Online, concert archives

### Category B — Estimates (⚠ verify before use):
- "Est. Value ($M)": insurance/estimated values for museum-owned works. Not auction-derived.
  These are rough order-of-magnitude for context; no authoritative public valuation exists.
- "Est. Copies Sold" for literary works: publisher estimates, literary databases. Round numbers.
- Paperback prices ($): approximate retail; varies by edition and retailer.

## Data Quality Classification

### Category A — Stable Historical Data:
- Art movement dates and key works: Grove Art Online, Oxford Art History
- Art prizes amounts: Turner Prize (tate.org.uk), Pritzker (pritzkerprize.com), MacArthur (macfound.org)
- Literature period dates and key works: Norton Anthology of Literature, standard academic consensus
- Philosophy works and dates: Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (plato.stanford.edu)
- Page counts: publisher data, commonly available editions

### Category B — Estimates (⚠ directional only):
- "Auction Peak ($M)" per movement: rough order-of-magnitude based on known record sales within each period.
  Not from a single authoritative source; use only for ballpark comparison.
- Edition prices ($): approximate contemporary retail prices; vary widely.

# Ethical Frameworks — Comparative Reference

## Framework Comparison Table

| Framework | Core Principle | Decision Criterion | Key Strength | Key Weakness | Primary Thinkers |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|--------------|--------------|-----------------|
| Act Utilitarianism | Maximize aggregate happiness (hedons) | Does this act produce the greatest good for the greatest number? | Quantifiable; democratic; flexible | Justifies harming minorities; requires impossible knowledge of all consequences | Bentham, early Mill |
| Rule Utilitarianism | Follow rules that maximize utility | Would following this rule generally maximize utility? | Avoids act-utilitarianism's extreme conclusions; stable expectations | Devolves into act-utilitarianism under pressure | Mill (later), Brandt |
| Kantian Deontology | Act from duty according to universal law | Can I universalize this maxim without contradiction? | Protects individual rights absolutely; consistent | Rigid; conflicting duties unresolvable; ignores consequences entirely | Kant |
| W.D. Ross's Prima Facie Duties | Multiple duties, not one supreme principle | Which duty is most binding given ALL duties in this situation? | More flexible than Kant; intuitively plausible | No systematic resolution when duties conflict | W.D. Ross |
| Virtue Ethics | Cultivate character to be a good person | What would a virtuous (phronimos) person do? | Holistic; contextual; focuses on character development | No action-guidance algorithm; who defines virtues? culturally variable | Aristotle, MacIntyre, Foot |
| Care Ethics | Maintain caring relationships and respond to vulnerability | Does this preserve relationships and attend to the particular needs of those involved? | Contextual; relational; challenges impartiality assumption | Hard to scale beyond personal relationships; can justify partiality | Gilligan, Noddings, Held |
| Contractarianism | Rules rational self-interested agents would agree to | Would self-interested rational agents agree to this rule? | Connects morality to rational self-interest | Ignores those outside the contract (animals, future generations, disabled) | Hobbes, Gauthier |
| Contractualism | Principles no one could reasonably reject | Could this principle be justified to everyone affected? | Non-aggregative; respects each person | "Reasonable rejection" undefined; vague threshold | Rawls, Scanlon |
| Natural Law | Morality derived from human nature/reason | Does this accord with our natural end (telos) and right reason? | Objective foundation; universal applicability claimed | Naturalistic fallacy risk; requires contested metaphysics | Aquinas, Finnis |

## Kant's Categorical Imperative — Three Formulations

| Formulation | Statement | Test |
|------------|-----------|------|
| Universal Law | "Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law" | Would universalizing this maxim produce a contradiction? |
| Humanity Formula | "Act so that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in that of another, always as an end and never as a means only" | Am I using a person purely instrumentally? |
| Kingdom of Ends | "Act according to maxims of a universally legislating member of a merely possible kingdom of ends" | Could rational agents agree to this rule in an ideal community? |

## Rawls' Theory of Justice — Key Elements

```
Original Position: hypothetical choice situation
Veil of Ignorance: don't know your place in society (class, race, gender, talents, conception of good)

Two Principles of Justice (lexical order):
1. Equal Basic Liberties Principle: each person has equal basic liberties compatible with same liberties for all
2. Difference Principle: social/economic inequalities must be:
   (a) attached to offices/positions open to all under fair equality of opportunity
   (b) arranged to benefit the least-advantaged members of society (maximin strategy)

Priority rule: Principle 1 > Principle 2(a) > Principle 2(b)
Critique (Nozick): Difference principle violates property rights; entitlement theory alternative
```

## Applied to Classic Dilemmas

### Trolley Problem (runaway trolley, 5 vs 1)

| Framework | Verdict | Reasoning |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| Act Utilitarianism | Pull lever (5 > 1) | maximize aggregate welfare |
| Kantian Deontology | DO NOT pull (contested) | using 1 person as means; or: duty to minimize harm (Ross: conflicting duties) |
| Virtue Ethics | Contextual; what would a person of practical wisdom do? | most virtue ethicists: pull lever; focus on agent's motive |
| Care Ethics | Who has relationships? Who is more vulnerable? | resists abstract calculation; focuses on particular relationships |
| Contractualism | Pull lever is permissible; nobody could reasonably reject the principle | Scanlon: but Footbridge variant (push fat man) is impermissible |

**Trolley vs Footbridge distinction (Thomson 1985):** physically pushing someone activates different intuitions than redirecting harm via lever; documented across cultures in empirical moral psychology.

### Heinz Dilemma (steal drug to save wife)

| Framework | Verdict |
|-----------|---------|
| Utilitarianism | Steal: wife's life > drug company's profit |
| Deontology (Kant) | Do not steal (cannot universalize theft); but also duty to preserve life → unresolvable |
| Virtue Ethics | A compassionate person steals; justice requires facing consequences after |
| Care Ethics | Steal: relationship to wife grounds the obligation |
| Contractualism | Drug company cannot reasonably reject a principle permitting theft under life-threatening necessity |

Kohlberg used this dilemma to measure moral development stages: pre-conventional → conventional → post-conventional.

### Corporate Whistleblowing

| Framework | Verdict |
|-----------|---------|
| Utilitarianism | Blow whistle if public harm > personal/company harm; requires estimating probabilities |
| Deontology | Duty of loyalty (contractual) vs duty to prevent harm; prima facie duty to prevent serious harm generally overrides |
| Virtue Ethics | An honest, courageous person speaks truth; integrity demands it |
| Care Ethics | To whom do you owe care? Colleagues at risk vs the public; particular relationships complicate the calculus |
| Contractualism | Nobody could reasonably reject a principle requiring disclosure of serious public harm |

**Source:** Rachels & Rachels "The Elements of Moral Philosophy" 9th ed. + Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy + Rawls "A Theory of Justice" revised ed. (1999)

# Logical Fallacies & Rhetoric Reference

## 30 Fallacies — Latin Name | Common Name | Definition | Example

| # | Latin Name | Common Name | Definition | Example |
|---|-----------|-------------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Ad hominem | Attack the person | Attack speaker instead of argument | "No le creas, tiene antecedentes penales" |
| 2 | Argumentum ad baculum | Appeal to force | Use threat instead of reason | "Agree or there'll be consequences" |
| 3 | Argumentum ad ignorantiam | Appeal to ignorance | "No proof it's false = must be true" | "Nobody has disproven ghosts exist" |
| 4 | Argumentum ad misericordiam | Appeal to pity | Emotion instead of evidence | "Give me an A, I studied so hard and I'm failing" |
| 5 | Argumentum ad populum | Bandwagon | Popularity implies truth | "100 million people can't be wrong" |
| 6 | Argumentum ad verecundiam | Appeal to authority | Expert in X cited for claim in Y | "Einstein believed in God" |
| 7 | Causa non causa | False cause | Correlation implies causation | "Crime went up after immigration increased" |
| 8 | Circulus in demonstrando | Circular reasoning | Conclusion hidden in premise | "The Bible is true because God wrote it" |
| 9 | Composition/Division | — | What's true of parts = true of whole (or reverse) | "Every player is good, so the team is great" |
| 10 | Cum hoc ergo propter hoc | Correlation = causation | A correlates with B → A causes B | "Ice cream sales correlate with drowning" |
| 11 | Dicto simpliciter | Hasty generalization | Small sample → universal rule | "I met two rude French people; all French are rude" |
| 12 | Equivocation | — | Same word used with different meanings | "I have a right to watch TV; what's right is good; watching TV is good" |
| 13 | False dichotomy | Either/or fallacy | Only 2 options when more exist | "You're either with us or against us" |
| 14 | Ignoratio elenchi | Red herring | Distract with irrelevant point | "Why worry about corruption when unemployment is high?" |
| 15 | Non sequitur | — | Conclusion doesn't follow from premises | "She's a good mother, so she'll be a great senator" |
| 16 | Petitio principii | Begging the question | Premise assumes what it's trying to prove | "God exists because the Bible says so" |
| 17 | Post hoc ergo propter hoc | Post hoc | A preceded B → A caused B | "I wore my lucky socks; we won" |
| 18 | Slippery slope | — | A will inevitably lead to extreme Z | "If we allow gay marriage, next people will marry animals" |
| 19 | Straw man | Hombre de paja | Distort opponent's position to attack weaker version | "You want fewer police? So you want anarchy?" |
| 20 | Tu quoque | Appeal to hypocrisy | "You do it too, so it's OK" | "Don't tell me to stop smoking; you drink" |
| 21 | Anecdotal | — | Single case overrides statistical evidence | "My grandfather smoked and lived to 95" |
| 22 | Gambler's fallacy | — | Past independent events affect future ones | "I've lost 5 times; I'm due for a win" |
| 23 | Sunk cost fallacy | — | Past investment justifies continuing bad decision | "We've spent €2M, we can't stop now" |
| 24 | Texas sharpshooter | — | Cherry-pick data cluster after the fact | "Look, all these cancer cases are near the plant" (drew circle after) |
| 25 | Appeal to nature | — | Natural = good; unnatural = bad | "Chemotherapy is unnatural, try herbal remedies" |
| 26 | Motte and bailey | — | Defend controversial (bailey) but retreat to trivial (motte) | "SJW ideology" ↔ "just believe in equality" |
| 27 | Nirvana fallacy | Perfect solution | Reject good solution because imperfect alternative exists | "Vaccines aren't 100% effective, why bother?" |
| 28 | False equivalence | — | Treat unequal things as equal | "Both sides have valid points" (one side: science; other: conspiracy) |
| 29 | Genetic fallacy | — | Dismiss argument based on its origin | "That theory was developed by Nazis, so it's wrong" |
| 30 | Burden of proof shift | — | Shift burden to skeptic | "Prove unicorns DON'T exist" |

## Toulmin Model (6 Components)

```
CLAIM: What you're arguing (the conclusion)
  ↑
DATA: Evidence/facts supporting the claim
  ↑
WARRANT: The implicit/explicit rule connecting data to claim
  ↑
BACKING: Support for the warrant itself (if challenged)
  ↑
QUALIFIER: Degree of certainty (usually, probably, in most cases)
  ↑
REBUTTAL: Conditions under which claim does NOT hold

Example:
  Claim: Aspirin reduces heart attack risk
  Data: Clinical trial RR=0.76 (95%CI 0.66-0.87)
  Warrant: Statistically significant RCT evidence is reliable
  Backing: Meta-analysis of 18 trials (Antithrombotic Trialists 2002)
  Qualifier: In high-risk patients
  Rebuttal: Unless GI bleeding risk outweighs benefit
```

## 15 Rhetorical Figures

| Figure | Definition | Example |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Anáfora | Repetition at start of successive clauses | "I have a dream... I have a dream..." |
| Metáfora | Implicit comparison; is = | "Life is a journey" |
| Símil | Explicit comparison; like/as | "He fought like a lion" |
| Hipérbole | Extreme exaggeration | "I've told you a million times" |
| Ironía | Meaning opposite to literal | "What lovely weather" (during storm) |
| Antítesis | Contrasting ideas in parallel | "Ask not what your country can do for you..." |
| Quiasmo | Inverted parallel structure (ABBA) | "You can take a horse to water, but you can't make it drink" |
| Paralelismo | Repeated grammatical structure | "Veni, vidi, vici" |
| Asíndeton | Omit conjunctions; staccato effect | "I came, I saw, I conquered" |
| Polisíndeton | Repeat conjunctions; slowing effect | "And the thunder... and the lightning... and the rain..." |
| Metonimia | Replace by associated concept | "The White House announced..." (= President) |
| Sinécdoque | Part for whole (or reverse) | "All hands on deck" (hands = sailors) |
| Personificación | Human qualities to non-human | "The economy is breathing again" |
| Elipsis | Omit words grammatically recoverable | "I'll have the steak [and] you [can have] the fish" |
| Eufemismo | Soften harsh truth | "Passed away" (= died); "downsizing" (= firing) |

**Source:** Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (plato.stanford.edu) + Perelman & Olbrechts-Tyteca "The New Rhetoric" (1958) + Corbett "Classical Rhetoric for the Modern Student"

# Narrative Structure — Beats & Reference

## Save the Cat — 15 Beats (110-page screenplay)

| Beat | Page | Function | Failure Mode |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| 1. Opening Image | 1 | Snapshot of "before world"; tone + theme | generic establishing shot |
| 2. Theme Stated | 5 | Someone (not hero) states the lesson; hero doesn't understand yet | explicit moralizing |
| 3. Set-Up | 1–10 | Hero, flaws, ordinary world, what needs to change, stakes | too much backstory |
| 4. Catalyst | 12 | Inciting incident; life changes irrevocably; hero receives the call | too late (past p.15) |
| 5. Debate | 12–25 | Should I go? Fear, preparation, false starts; internal conflict | skipping doubt entirely |
| 6. Break Into Two | 25 | Hero's active choice to enter new world; end Act 1 | passive hero dragged in |
| 7. B Story | 30 | Subplot begins (usually romance or mentor); carries theme | no thematic purpose |
| 8. Fun and Games | 30–55 | The premise's "promise"; what the trailer sells; upward path | no escalating fun |
| 9. Midpoint | 55 | False victory OR false defeat; stakes raised; hero "thinks" they've won/lost | nothing actually changes |
| 10. Bad Guys Close In | 55–75 | External pressure grows; internal team fractures | purely external conflict only |
| 11. All Is Lost | 75 | Lowest point; "whiff of death" (literal or symbolic) | too mild; hero recovers too fast |
| 12. Dark Night of the Soul | 75–85 | Emotional rock-bottom; old way has failed; moment of despair | skipped or 2 pages |
| 13. Break Into Three | 85 | A-ha synthesis of A+B story; hero finds new solution/perspective | epiphany unexplained |
| 14. Finale | 85–110 | New plan; storm the castle; high stakes action; catharsis; world changed | montage instead of earned |
| 15. Final Image | 110 | Mirror of Opening Image; shows transformation; proof of theme | identical to opening |

## Hero's Journey (Campbell/Vogler — 12 Stages)

| Stage | Act | Core Action |
|-------|-----|------------|
| Ordinary World | 1 | Hero's normal life established |
| Call to Adventure | 1 | Problem/challenge presented |
| Refusal of the Call | 1 | Fear; reasons to stay |
| Meeting the Mentor | 1 | Guide provides tools/wisdom |
| Crossing the Threshold | 1→2 | Commits to adventure; no return |
| Tests, Allies, Enemies | 2 | Rules of new world learned |
| Approach to the Inmost Cave | 2 | Prep for major ordeal |
| The Ordeal | 2 | Crisis; hero faces death/fear |
| Reward (Seizing the Sword) | 2 | Survives; gains treasure/insight |
| The Road Back | 2→3 | Return journey; stakes raised |
| Resurrection | 3 | Final test; fully transformed |
| Return with the Elixir | 3 | Brings back gift for ordinary world |

## Show Don't Tell — 10 Patterns

| Tell | Show |
|------|------|
| She was angry | She slammed the folder onto the desk, scattering papers across the floor |
| He was nervous | He checked his phone three times in sixty seconds |
| They were in love | She laughed at his terrible jokes; he memorized how she took her coffee |
| The room was filthy | Three empty pizza boxes competed for space with a mountain of unread mail |
| He was powerful | When he walked in, the conversation stopped |
| She was exhausted | She fell asleep mid-sentence, pen still in hand |
| It was cold | He saw his breath and wished he'd worn the other coat |
| They had a bad marriage | They sat through dinner without looking up from their phones |
| The city was dangerous | No one made eye contact. Everyone walked fast |
| He was lying | He looked at the clock twice before answering |

## Character Arc — Three Types

| Arc Type | Beginning | End | Example |
|----------|-----------|-----|---------|
| Positive (Transformation) | flawed, incomplete | overcomes flaw | Walter White (early), Scrooge |
| Negative (Corruption) | moral, good | corrupts/falls | Breaking Bad's Walter White |
| Flat (Test) | already complete | tested, holds values | James Bond, Sherlock Holmes |

**Wound → Lie → Want vs Need:**
- Wound: past trauma that shapes worldview
- Lie: false belief the character holds because of the wound
- Want: conscious goal driving action
- Need: unconscious truth the character must learn; theme of the story

Compelling antagonist rule: the antagonist must believe they are right. Mirror-function: antagonist embodies what the protagonist could become if they don't change.

## Scene-Level Pacing

```
Scene must accomplish minimum 2 of:
1. Advance plot
2. Reveal character
3. Establish/shift relationship
4. Introduce/escalate conflict
5. Establish tone/theme

Scene turns on conflict: enter late, leave early
Goal → Conflict → Outcome (rarely what was wanted)
```

**Source:** Blake Snyder "Save the Cat" (2005) + Vogler "The Writer's Journey" 3rd ed. (2007) + McKee "Story" (1997)

# Translation Techniques Reference

## Vinay & Darbelnet's 7 Procedures

| # | Technique | Definition | EN → ES Example |
|---|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Borrowing (préstamo) | Use source word unchanged | software → software; marketing → marketing |
| 2 | Calque | Literal structure translation; new compound | skyscraper → rascacielos; football → fútbol americano |
| 3 | Literal translation | Word-for-word; grammatical structure preserved | The book is on the table → El libro está en la mesa |
| 4 | Transposition | Change grammatical category; meaning preserved | He swam across the river → Cruzó el río a nado (verb → adverb) |
| 5 | Modulation | Change point of view / perspective | It's not hard → Es fácil; The glass is half empty → el vaso está medio lleno |
| 6 | Equivalence | Culturally equivalent expression; non-literal | It's raining cats and dogs → Llueve a cántaros |
| 7 | Adaptation | Cultural substitution; target culture equivalent | Baseball analogy → fútbol analogy in generic sports context |

**Hierarchy of procedures:** Literal (when possible) → Transposition → Modulation → Equivalence → Adaptation (last resort, highest domestication)

## 50 False Friends EN ↔ ES

| English Word | Meaning | Spanish FALSE friend | Correct Translation |
|--------------|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| actually | in fact, really | actualmente (= currently) | de hecho, en realidad |
| argument | quarrel, dispute | argumento (= reasoning/plot) | discusión, pelea |
| assist | help | asistir (= attend) | ayudar |
| billion | 10⁹ | billón (= 10¹²) | mil millones |
| carpet | floor covering | carpeta (= folder/portfolio) | alfombra |
| college | university | colegio (= school K-12) | universidad |
| comedian | comic actor | comediante (pejorative in some contexts) | cómico, humorista |
| compromise | mutual concession | comprometer (= to commit/endanger) | acuerdo, concesión mutua |
| conductor | orchestra leader; train staff | conductor (= driver) | director de orquesta; revisor |
| contest | competition | contestar (= to answer) | concurso, competición |
| deception | dishonest trick | decepción (= disappointment) | engaño |
| disgrace | shame | desgracia (= misfortune) | vergüenza, deshonra |
| editorial | newspaper opinion piece | editorial (= publishing house) | artículo de opinión |
| embarrassed | ashamed | embarazada (= pregnant) | avergonzado/a |
| eventually | ultimately, in the end | eventualmente (= possibly, occasionally) | finalmente, al final |
| exit | way out | éxito (= success) | salida |
| fabric | cloth, textile | fábrica (= factory) | tela, tejido |
| facilities | amenities, infrastructure | facilidades (= ease, concessions) | instalaciones |
| genial | friendly, cordial | genial (= brilliant, great) | afable, simpático |
| gross | disgusting; 144 items | grosero (= rude) | repugnante; bruto (gross income) |
| idiom | fixed expression | idioma (= language) | expresión idiomática, modismo |
| incident | event, occurrence | incidente (= altercation) | acontecimiento, suceso |
| inhabit | to live in | inhibir (= to inhibit) | habitar |
| injury | physical wound | injuria (= insult) | herida, lesión |
| integrate | combine | íntegro (= upright, whole) | integrar |
| large | big, great in size | largo (= long) | grande |
| lecture | academic talk | lectura (= reading) | conferencia, clase |
| library | book repository | librería (= bookshop) | biblioteca |
| luxury | extravagance | lujuria (= lust) | lujo |
| mayor | city leader | mayor (= bigger, older) | alcalde |
| misery | suffering | miseria (= poverty, meanness) | sufrimiento, dolor |
| notice | observe; notification | noticia (= news item) | aviso; fijarse en |
| once | one time | once (= eleven) | una vez |
| ordinary | common, unremarkable | ordinario (= vulgar, rude) | común, corriente |
| parents | mother and father | parientes (= relatives) | padres |
| particular | specific; fussy | particular (= private, individual) | específico, concreto |
| pretend | feign, make-believe | pretender (= to intend, to court) | fingir, simular |
| propaganda | biased information | propaganda (= advertisement) | propaganda (same, but wider use in ES) |
| realize | become aware of | realizar (= to carry out, make) | darse cuenta de |
| record | document; best achievement | récord (= record/best mark) | grabar (to record audio); registro |
| resume | CV / to continue | resumen (= summary) | currículum; reanudar |
| rope | thick cord | ropa (= clothing) | cuerda, soga |
| rude | impolite | rudo (= rough, tough) | grosero, maleducado |
| sanity | mental health | sanidad (= health system) | cordura, salud mental |
| sensible | reasonable, practical | sensible (= sensitive, emotional) | sensato, razonable |
| sensitive | emotionally aware | sensitivo (= sensory) | sensible |
| suburb | residential area outside city | suburbio (= slum area) | barrio residencial, extrarradio |
| success | achievement | suceso (= event, incident) | éxito |
| sympathetic | showing compassion | simpático (= likeable, friendly) | comprensivo, empático |
| ultimately | in the end | últimamente (= lately, recently) | en última instancia, finalmente |

## 40 Discourse Connectors EN ↔ ES

| Category | English | Spanish |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **Addition** | moreover | además, es más |
| | furthermore | asimismo, es más |
| | in addition (to) | además de |
| | what is more | encima, es más |
| | not only... but also | no solo... sino también |
| **Contrast** | however | sin embargo |
| | nevertheless | no obstante |
| | nonetheless | a pesar de ello |
| | whereas | mientras que, en cambio |
| | although / even though | aunque, si bien |
| | in spite of / despite | a pesar de |
| | on the other hand | por otro lado |
| **Cause** | because | porque, ya que |
| | since | puesto que, dado que |
| | due to | debido a |
| | owing to | a causa de |
| | as a result of | como resultado de |
| **Consequence** | therefore | por lo tanto |
| | consequently | en consecuencia |
| | thus | así pues, por ende |
| | hence | de ahí que |
| | as a result | como resultado, por tanto |
| **Exemplification** | for example | por ejemplo |
| | for instance | a modo de ejemplo |
| | such as | tal(es) como |
| | namely | a saber, esto es |
| **Concession** | admittedly | es cierto que, hay que reconocer que |
| | granted | concedido que |
| | it is true that | es verdad que |
| **Reformulation** | in other words | en otras palabras |
| | that is (to say) | es decir |
| | to put it differently | dicho de otro modo |
| **Summary** | in conclusion | en conclusión |
| | to sum up | en resumen |
| | in short | en pocas palabras |
| | all in all | en definitiva |
| **Sequence** | first(ly), second(ly) | primero, segundo, en primer lugar |
| | then, next | luego, a continuación |
| | finally | finalmente, por último |
| | subsequently | posteriormente |

**Source:** Vinay & Darbelnet "Stylistique comparée du français et de l'anglais" (1958, English translation 1995) + RAE Diccionario Panhispánico de Dudas + Mona Baker "In Other Words: A Coursebook on Translation" 3rd ed. (2018)
